speaker: interviewer
text: Robert, you were telling us about Bauer, the-- your father's assistant or-- in World War I.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He was actually the servant of my father. In the Austrian Imperial Army, an officer had always a servant, because an officer was not supposed to clean his shoes or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this Bauer and my father were always in contact with. And when the economic situation in the early '20s in Austria was very bad, he wanted to emigrate to Paraguay.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why Paraguay?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because in Paraguay, farmers were needed. And the Paraguayan government made commitments to Austrian farmers. If they would come over and farm certain lands for 10 years, they would give them the land. And-- and a colony, a German-Austrian colony, existed in Villarrica, Paraguay. And my father made it possible for Bauer and his family to emigrate from Austria to Paraguay.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he have a large family?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had a very large family. And not knowing, he actually made his fortune with his family, because he had, I believe, five or six daughters. And three of the daughters were in the age of-- marriageable age. And when they came to Paraguay, they found many young Austrians there without wives and without the possibilities of getting a European girl. And suddenly, they found family a with the girls coming in. And of course, they were married in very short time. And Bauer had all the help he needed to build up his farm. And he was very grateful for the help my father could provide to him. And matter of fact, he wrote once he should-- my father should send him a special water pump. And it took big efforts to get the water pump bought and sent from Austria to Paraguay.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when the time came of the release from prison, my father immediately got in contact to-- with Bauer in Paraguay and asked him to send him a [NON-ENGLISH]. A [NON-ENGLISH] is an official immigration document similar to an-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] certificate to go to Palestine or a quota number to come to the United States. And Bauer immediately sent him a [NON-ENGLISH] for my father, my mother, and the two children.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we would have been able to emigrate from Vienna almost immediately, because in the meantime we made our passports, which were stamped with a large J, meaning Jew-- Jew-- Jew. And we got also all the necessary travel documents. And we even had the-- the ship tickets. I remember it was the HAPAG going from Hamburg to Montevideo and then Montevideo up the Parana to Asuncion in Paraguay. But suddenly, something came up. It came that my father had, in Switzerland, in the little town of Wil, not far--

speaker: interviewer
text: Spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Wil, W-E-- W-I-L, not far from St. Gallen-- machineries producing [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] lace. And Swiss farmers, during the wintertime when the-- when they had very little work to do, produced lace on large, long, special machineries for him. And of course, he had also money, Swiss money, in the-- in Swiss banks. He has-- he had machinery. And he had an-- a commerce between Switzerland and Austria. And when the Germans took the business away, they wanted this particular machinery, which was a very delicate and special piece of equipment, transferred from Switzerland, from Wil, to Austria. And also, they wanted the Swiss franc, which were in Swiss banks, transferred to Austria.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they did allow only out of four passports two passports for emigration. And because my father had this experience of imprisonment, and because my brother is two years older than me, at that time 19 years old, and because we, as a family, thought that they are the most-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the-- the people in bigger danger, we decided they should-- they should go first. The Germans didn't want your father to remain to execute this transfer of funds and machinery?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were very happy that my mother would be here doing that, because my mother worked--

speaker: interviewer
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --With my father in-- in the business. And my-- and my mother agreed to that.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had such details on individuals that if a guy came into the consulate or wherever, the-- the passport house, that they would know his business? They would know what he did, what he owned, what they wanted from him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Don't forget my father had a registered business. And the books were always under control of CPAs. When the Germans took the books-- when the-- took the business over, the books were available to them. All the records of export and import between Switzerland were-- were needed. The bank books were in their hands, not in ours. The only need they had was signatures, actually, to transfer money, although time was necessary too. Machinery couldn't be shipped from day to the next. Unfortunately, what happened was that the war with Poland started. My father and brother left. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The war with Poland was in September of '39, and they left-- I would have to look it up exactly, but they left in March of '39, so they left a few months earlier.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I should have mentioned that-- that the real persecution of the Jews started with the Crystal Night in Vienna and in Germany too. That was the time when the Germans really tighten up everything what was in Jewish hands.

speaker: interviewer
text: That would have been November, beginning of November 1938, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. I forgot to mention that Jewish books were burned, that Jewish intellectuals were imprisoned immediately, that [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] anything what you can think of was difficult. A Jewish woman was only allowed to go shopping between 3:00 and 5:00. And suddenly, things which were on the shelves disappeared. They were not sold to them. I remember, as a child I couldn't go to a barber shop to have my hair cut. I had to go to a Jewish barber shop.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the regular barber shop wouldn't cut Jewish hair, contaminating his scissors. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Times-- almost overnight things started to change. Public transportation was strictly regulated. You couldn't travel anymore freely within Austria. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] For everything documents were required. We had part of our family in Czechoslovakia, and we would have loved to go there, but it was impossible. They couldn't visit us either. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me. Were they in eastern or western Czechoslovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were in a part of Moravia, which is the western part of Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had they not been affected by Kristallnacht in western Czechoslovakia? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had been affected too, yes. Of course. What I wanted to say is free travel didn't exist anymore. We couldn't move. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Financially, the situation was very difficult for us too, because we suddenly found ourselves without money. We were-- after my father and brother left, we were required to go into what was called the Viennese ghetto. The 2nd and 20th district was used to concentrate all the Viennese Jews living out in the other districts. And I had to go and live with an uncle of mine. My mother, in order to earn money, had to go to-- to work in households of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] more-- better-situated Jewish families. And I took a few courses given by the Jewish community in order to learn trades. And I also started to get in contact with the Jewish youth groups, because don't forget, we couldn't go into parks. We couldn't go into the Vienna Woods. We couldn't ride the public transportations. We had actually nothing to do. So we met into-- we met in the home of the Jewish youth groups, which was in the 1st district in the Marc-Aurel-Strasse 5. And it was called JUL.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: J-U-L. Jewish-- Jugen Youth Group or something like that. And every Jewish youth group had a little room there, and we met. And that is actually the first time I met Aron Menczer. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is a man?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That was an-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a young man. I believe he was perhaps five or six years older than me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you say his name and spell it for us again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Aron, A-R-O-N. Menczer is M-E-N-Z-E-R. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we belonged to different groups. There was Betar, and there was Hashomer Hatzair, and there was Gordonia, and there was you know, Mizrachi. But for us, that was not very important. And I joined the Hashomer Hatzair not because I really liked it, because my friends-- my friends were there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was this Aron Menczer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Aron Menczer was an student. He-- he was an Jewish-- I don't want to say intellectual, but he was an well-educated young man, which made it-- which made it his business to run or supervise those Jewish children which came to the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] JUL, came to this building, which was set up by the Jewish community as an recreational center for the children, for the Jewish children, so the children are not always at home. And they couldn't be on the streets, so they should be in certain classrooms. And Aron Menczer had the idea of teaching them, first of all, Judaism, because most of those children were like me, from assimilated families. And that was the first time when we heard about Jewish songs; when we learned how to dance the  hora, a traditional Jewish dance; when we heard about Jewish writers; or when we heard about the history of immigration into Palestine, the first [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] building of kibbutzim. And he slowly introduced us to the idea of Zionism. He was instrumental in creating a garden, or taking over a garden, which was owned by Rothschild in the outskirts of Vienna, and made a small beginning of a hachshara.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is a hachshara?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hachshara is an-- is the idea of group teaching children or teenagers how to live from the land, which means work in the garden, produce vegetables, have perhaps a cow, how to milk a cow, which was very strange for us. And by the way, the vegetables produced in this particular garden went to the Jewish hospital called Billroth Hospital in Vienna, and where we actually did productive work because of that. In the meantime, many things happened. Suddenly I found out an uncle or an aunt were gone. They were just emigrating to Australia. Or another one was taking the opportunity and went to the United-- had the opportunity to go to the United States. That was-- I am-- I'm talking about the time just before the-- the war in Poland, 1939. So slowly, part of my family disappeared.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were-- were there many Austrian-- Viennese Jews or Austrian Jews who were able to emigrate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Many who had visas?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In matter of fact, more than half of the original 200,000 managed to leave Austria before September of 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of my family left. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I know that records were kept. And of course, suddenly you saw an emptiness around you. Faces you knew were gone. And when you asked what happened with so-and-so, oh, he got the affidavit or he got on a children's-- he got on a children's transport to England or to Sweden. Some of those people unfortunately left and were caught a second time, because they left and went to Hungary, or they left and went to Czechoslovakia, or left and went to Yugoslavia. Some of them had relatives, which made it possible to them. So a large portion of people left. I had also a cousin working in the [NON-ENGLISH] in Vienna. And he worked in a particular department of the [NON-ENGLISH], which had all the records of the Jewish people of Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Ernst Weiss. And me having nowhere to go, I lived with his father and mother in his-- in their apartment. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And one day he came to me and he said, Bob, the Germans requested lists from us. I think it would be a good idea if you would go and voluntarily [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] say you want to go into the Arbeitsdienst, which is called workers-- that you want to work for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: For the Germans.

speaker: interviewee
text: For the Germans. And I did this. And I was-- I got an-- a postcard. I had to go to the Gestapo headquarters in the Prinz-Eugen-Strasse on a certain date, on a certain time, which I did. I was, I believe, not even 17 at that time. And suddenly I found myself in the courtyard assembled with perhaps close to 400 other Jews. And a very famous German-- I shouldn't say German, he was an Austrian-- SS man came, by the name of Eichmann. And he held a little speech, and he said now you-- you will-- to the assembled Jews, now you will enter the trucks on the outside, and we will bring you to the labor camps we have-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we have opened up for you. And one was on the Loibl Pass in Carinthia, I remember. And another one was in another small town. I have the name on my lips, but right now I cannot find the name. And a list was read. And suddenly the 400 people slowly were loaded up on trucks and the trucks left. And left over standing in the courtyard was another [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] young fellow. And the only thing what I remember about him was that he had only nine fingers. That was another thing interesting. He said, show us your hands. You never worked in your life. Now we will teach you how to work. That was Eichmann--

speaker: interviewer
text: Eichmann--

speaker: interviewee
text: --which say this to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: --said this to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: To all us.

speaker: interviewer
text: You remained.

speaker: interviewee
text: All of us. And that is the-- that is the way I remember the young fellow next to me. When he showed his hands, he had only nine fingers. I don't know his name, but I will never forget his hands. And to us too he say, you are the only ones which volunteered, and you will work in Vienna. And we got working papers. The next day I had to go to a construction company. And I remember the-- the first job I had was in the-- in a building which had water in the cellar. And they took the water out. That was the [SPEAKING GERMAN] in Vienna. That was a government building. And later on, we worked in different-- in different other buildings. The situation about food, I wanted to tell you also, became very critical. Everything was on strict ration cards. And the Jewish ration cards were marked J, a red J. And many things the German or the Austrian were able to purchase were not free for the Jews. We were also very restricted on the time of-- we could go into the-- into the stores. And-- and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] money was very scarce for a lot of people at the time, because the Germans took a lot of money. They took all our jewelry. We had to give up all jewelry. The only thing what a woman was left to wear was the wedding band.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the-- the Austrian Jewish community made soup kitchens. And the soup kitchens were for them available in the hospitals and the old age homes. And they used them and they fed, actually, the Jewish population in Vienna. And I remember every day, once a day, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you picked up with a little pail your food ration. Who funded that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a-- that's a very good-- very good question. In Austria, there were also very rich Jews. When they left, they could only-- they were only allowed to take 10 marks with them. So the-- so a suggestion was made to them, perhaps a little bit of forceful one from the Jewish community, that you have to contribute your money you cannot take anyway with you to the support of those Jews which don't have any more financial-- financial help.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. We're going to continue this on the following tape so we don't interrupt you. Thank you.
